content.method: Structured comparative case-process tracing of Finland's 1972-1990s reform sequence against Hungary's current fiscal capacity, teacher supply, and curriculum governance data; expert elicitation with Hungarian and Finnish education-policy specialists to assess transferability of each component.
content.question: Which specific institutional features (funding equalization, teacher qualification requirements, curriculum mandates, nearby-school assignment defaults) were most critical to Finland's success in containing segregation after comprehensive reform, and which of these are currently fiscally and administratively feasible for Hungary to implement alongside or instead of structural detracking?
content.why_it_matters: The Finnish case is repeatedly cited as both model and cautionary tale: structural reform alone was insufficient without two decades of complementary policy bundling. This determines whether TP-live-t2 (structural reform) needs to be paired with TP-live-t4-style investment and TP-live-t3-style admissions reform to avoid replicating Finland's later erosion, or whether Hungary's fiscal/capacity constraints make the full Finnish bundle infeasible.
